definition: Alcohol By Volume, the standard international measure of alcohol content expressed as a percentage. Most whiskey is bottled between 40% and 46% ABV, though cask strength releases can exceed 60% ABV.

The number on a whisky bottle indicating the youngest spirit in the blend, expressed in years. By law, the age statement must reflect the youngest component. NAS (No Age Statement) whiskies omit this number entirely.

The portion of whiskey that evaporates from the cask during maturation, typically 1-2% per year in Scotland and up to 4-5% in warmer climates like Kentucky. Over decades of aging, a significant volume is lost to evaporation.

The highest price achieved for a bottle of whiskey at public auction. Rare Scotch whiskies, particularly from closed distilleries, dominate record prices, with some bottles selling for millions of dollars.

A whisky created by blending single malts from two or more distilleries, without adding grain whisky. In Japan, this practice is less common because most distilleries are owned by the same company and rarely trade casks.
A whisky created by combining single malt whiskies from multiple distilleries with grain whisky for consistency and balance. Blended Scotch accounts for over 90% of all Scotch whisky sold worldwide.

In the United States, a blend of at least 20% straight whiskey with neutral grain spirits or other whiskey. Not to be confused with blended Scotch, which combines malt and grain whiskies but does not include neutral spirits.

A government-supervised warehouse where Irish whiskey must age for at least three years in wooden casks on the island of Ireland. The bonded status ensures tax is paid only when the whiskey leaves for bottling.

The physical state of a collectible whiskey bottle, including fill level, label integrity, capsule condition, and overall presentation. Condition significantly affects secondary market value, with pristine bottles commanding substantial premiums.

A designation under the Bottled-in-Bond Act of 1897 requiring whiskey to be the product of one distiller, one distillery, one distilling season, aged at least four years in a federally bonded warehouse, and bottled at exactly 100 proof (50% ABV).

An American whiskey made from a mash bill of at least 51% corn, distilled to no more than 160 proof, entered into new charred oak barrels at no more than 125 proof, and bottled at minimum 80 proof. Despite popular belief, bourbon can be made anywhere in the United States.

The Kentucky Bourbon Trail, a tourism program featuring guided tours of major bourbon distilleries across Kentucky. Established in 1999, it now includes over 40 distilleries and attracts over 2 million visitors annually.

The practice of transferring mature whiskey to a different type of cask for a secondary period of aging. Common finishes include sherry, port, rum, wine, and Madeira casks, each adding new flavor dimensions.

Purchasing a full cask of maturing whiskey as an investment, with the intention of bottling or reselling after further aging. Legitimate cask investment requires verified warehouse storage, insurance, and transparent exit strategies.

A unique identifier assigned to each cask at the time of filling, used for traceability and single cask bottling. Single cask releases proudly display this number as proof of provenance and uniqueness.

Whiskey bottled directly from the cask without dilution, typically ranging from 50% to 65% ABV depending on aging conditions. Cask strength bottlings preserve the full intensity and complexity developed during maturation.

The process of setting the inside of a new oak barrel on fire for a controlled duration, creating a layer of charcoal that acts as a flavor filter and caramelizes wood sugars. Char levels range from #1 (light, 15 seconds) to #4 (alligator char, 55 seconds).

A process of cooling whiskey to near-freezing temperatures and passing it through fine filters to remove fatty acids and proteins that cause haziness when chilled or diluted. Non-chill-filtered whiskies retain more texture and flavor but may appear cloudy.

A distillery that has permanently ceased production, making its remaining whiskey increasingly rare and valuable over time. Famous closed distilleries include Port Ellen, Rosebank, and Brora, though some have been revived.

A continuous distillation apparatus, also called a Coffey still or patent still, that processes wash in a continuous stream rather than batches. Column stills produce a lighter, higher-proof spirit and are standard for grain whiskey and bourbon production.

The apparatus that cools alcohol vapors back into liquid form after distillation. Shell-and-tube condensers produce a cleaner spirit, while traditional worm tub condensers preserve more copper contact and create a heavier, more characterful distillate.

The interaction between spirit vapors and copper surfaces in the still, which strips undesirable sulfur compounds from the distillate. Greater copper contact from taller stills or longer lyne arms generally produces a lighter, fruitier spirit.

The modern movement of small, independent distilleries producing artisanal whiskey, emerging from the early 2000s. The craft revival has dramatically expanded the diversity of whiskey styles, ingredients, and production philosophies worldwide.

A loosely defined term for whiskey produced by small, independent distilleries, typically emphasizing artisanal methods, local ingredients, and hands-on production. No legal definition exists in most countries, making the term open to interpretation.

The process of heating a fermented liquid to separate alcohol from water based on differing boiling points. Whiskey is typically distilled two or three times in copper pot stills or column stills to concentrate alcohol and flavor compounds.

A traditional Scottish term for a measure of whisky, used colloquially to mean any glass or pour. Historically a dram was one-eighth of a fluid ounce, but today it simply means a sociable serving of whisky.

Spirit caramel, a food coloring additive permitted in Scotch whisky and many other whiskies to ensure batch-to-batch color consistency. While legal in most countries, its use is controversial and must not affect flavor. Some distillers proudly state 'no color added.'
The final fraction of a distillation run, also called tails, containing heavier alcohols and oily compounds. Feints are typically recycled into the next distillation batch rather than discarded.

The biological process where yeast converts sugars in the wort into alcohol and carbon dioxide, typically lasting 48 to 96 hours in large wooden or steel washbacks. The resulting liquid, called wash, reaches roughly 7-10% ABV.

The amount of liquid remaining in a sealed whiskey bottle, which can decrease over decades through evaporation via the cork. Base of neck, upper shoulder, and mid shoulder are standard descriptors, with lower levels reducing value.

The lingering taste and sensation after swallowing whiskey, measured both in duration (short, medium, long) and character (dry, sweet, warming, spicy). A long, complex finish is generally considered a mark of quality.

A cask being used for the first time to mature whiskey after its initial purpose (such as holding bourbon or sherry). First fill casks impart the strongest wood influence, contributing intense flavors of vanilla, fruit, or spice.

The traditional method of malting barley by spreading it on a stone floor and manually turning it with shovels during germination. Only a handful of distilleries still practice floor malting, including Laphroaig and Balvenie.
The first fraction of distillate collected during a spirit run, containing volatile compounds like methanol and acetone that are toxic and undesirable. Foreshots are always discarded by the stillman.

A legal designation that protects the name of a product originating from a specific region. Scotch whisky, Irish whiskey, bourbon, and Japanese whisky all carry geographic indications that govern where and how they may be produced.

Irish whiskey produced in a column still from a mash of corn or wheat with a small percentage of malted barley. Lighter and sweeter than pot still whiskey, Irish grain whiskey forms the backbone of blended Irish whiskey.

The middle fraction of a distillation run, selected by the stillman for its optimal balance of flavor, purity, and alcohol content. Only the hearts — also called the middle cut — are collected for maturation into whiskey.

A simple cocktail of whisky and soda water served over ice, traditionally in a tall glass. The highball is a cultural institution in Japan, where meticulous preparation and precise ice-to-whisky ratios elevate the format to an art form.

The largest Scotch whisky region by geography, spanning from the central lowlands to the northern coast. Highland malts vary enormously in style, from light and floral to rich, full-bodied, and maritime.

A company that purchases casks of whisky from distilleries and bottles them under its own label, often as single cask releases. Notable independent bottlers include Gordon & MacPhail, Signatory, and Cadenhead's.

A Scottish island off the west coast known for intensely peated, smoky whiskies. The island's nine distilleries, including Laphroaig, Ardbeg, and Lagavulin, produce some of the world's most distinctive and polarizing single malts.

Voluntary standards introduced in 2021 by the Japan Spirits & Liqueurs Makers Association requiring Japanese whisky to be fermented, distilled, and aged in Japan for at least three years. Prior to these standards, labeling was largely unregulated.

The streaks of liquid that form on the inside of a whiskey glass after swirling, also called tears or curtains. While often attributed to quality, legs actually indicate alcohol content and viscosity rather than overall excellence.

A whiskey release produced in restricted quantities, often from a single cask or small batch, and typically not repeated. Limited editions command premium prices and are sought after by collectors for their scarcity and unique character.

The pipe connecting the head of a pot still to the condenser, also called the lye pipe. The angle of the lyne arm affects reflux: an upward angle promotes lighter spirits by forcing heavier vapors back down, while a downward angle creates richer distillate.

The process of soaking barley in water, allowing it to germinate, then drying it in a kiln to halt growth. Malting activates enzymes that convert starch to sugar during mashing, and the kiln fuel determines whether the malt is peated or unpeated.

The recipe of grains used to produce a whiskey, expressed as a percentage of each grain type. Bourbon mash bills typically combine corn, rye or wheat, and malted barley. The mash bill is the primary driver of a whiskey's flavor profile.

The process of mixing milled grain with hot water in a mash tun to convert starches into fermentable sugars. Temperature rests activate enzymes that break down the grain, producing a sweet liquid called wort.

The head of quality and blending at a whiskey company, responsible for maintaining consistency across releases and creating new expressions. Master blenders train their palates over decades and are considered among the industry's most important figures.

Crushing malted barley through a roller mill to produce grist, a mix of flour, grits, and husks. The grist ratio is critical: too fine and the mash tun clogs, too coarse and sugar extraction is inefficient.
